skill.name: cookbook-audit
skill.description: Audit Anthropic Cookbook notebooks against a style rubric, automated validation checks, and quality standards.
skill.summary: Review Cookbook notebooks for narrative quality, code quality, technical accuracy, and publication readiness.
skill.author: anthropics

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, environment, network, and weak-crypto patterns. AI review confirms one high-risk shell command injection issue in validate_notebook.py; most other hits are documentation examples, generated audit text, or informational URLs. No prompt injection or confirmed malicious intent was found, but publication should wait until the unsafe shell command construction is fixed.

high_findings.title: Shell Command Injection in Secret Scan Pipeline
high_findings.description: validate_notebook.py builds a sh -c command with a formatted notebook path and then executes it. Because the notebook path comes from user input, a crafted filename containing a quote or shell syntax could escape the echo command and run arbitrary commands. This is a true positive even though the intended tool is detect-secrets.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess call invokes sh -c and interpolates notebook_abs into the shell string. The path is derived from sys.argv, so command injection is possible with a crafted filename.

medium_findings.title: External Tool Execution Requires Trust Boundaries
medium_findings.description: The validator runs uv, jupyter nbconvert, uvx, and detect-secrets against notebooks. The nbconvert command uses argument lists, which is safer, but these tools still execute local binaries and may download packages. This is legitimate for a notebook audit skill but should be disclosed and constrained.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The external command execution is explicit and semantically required by the skill. The risk is elevated because it operates on user-selected files and invokes package tooling.

low_findings.title: Environment and Secret Key Matches Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The env_access and API key hits in SKILL.md and style_guide.md describe good and bad notebook practices. They do not read this skill runtime environment or exfiltrate credentials. These are false positives, but they remain relevant because the skill reviews notebooks that may contain secrets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are checklist items or fenced examples for teaching dotenv and avoiding hardcoded keys. No actual credential read or network send appears at those locations.

low_findings.title: Network URL Matches Are Informational
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to the Anthropic Console and the uv project page. They are shown as documentation or usage help, not used for outbound requests by the skill code. These network findings are false positives.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The style guide URL is a user-facing API key link, and the validate_notebook.py URL is printed in help text. There is no request, fetch, socket, or HTTP client call at these locations.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography and Reconnaissance Matches Are Lexical False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm and system reconnaissance hits occur in markdown, generated audit text, model guidance, or normal validation logic. I found no hashing, encryption, credential cracking, or host reconnaissance behavior in the reviewed context.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are descriptions, comments, or notebook validation checks rather than cryptographic or reconnaissance operations. The pattern appears to be triggered by text tokens rather than executable security-sensitive behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: sh -c With Interpolated User-Controlled Path
dangerous_patterns.description: A shell string is assembled with the notebook path and executed through subprocess.run. Replace the shell pipeline with argument-list subprocess calls or pass the notebook path through stdin without shell interpretation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The dangerous pattern is directly visible: sh -c receives an f-string containing notebook_abs. The notebook path is supplied by the command line.

content.user_title: Audit Cookbook notebooks with a rubric
content.value_statement: Cookbook notebooks need consistent review before publication. This skill combines automated notebook checks with a structured rubric for clear, actionable audit reports.
actual_capabilities: Runs a Python validator against a provided Jupyter notebook path.
Converts notebook content to markdown for easier manual review.
Checks for hardcoded secrets using detect-secrets or fallback patterns.
Reviews introductions, prerequisites, conclusions, model constants, and output hygiene.
Scores narrative quality, code quality, technical accuracy, and actionability.
Produces prioritized recommendations with examples from the reviewed notebook.
limitations: It is designed for Anthropic Cookbook notebooks, not arbitrary documentation.
It does not execute notebook cells to prove runtime correctness.
It depends on local tools such as uv, nbconvert, and detect-secrets.
Manual judgment is still required for pedagogy, clarity, and technical accuracy.

use_cases.title: Pre-publication quality review
use_cases.description: Check whether a submitted notebook meets style, structure, security, and scoring expectations before merging.

use_cases.title: Consistent editorial feedback
use_cases.description: Use the rubric to produce repeatable feedback on narrative flow, learning objectives, setup, and conclusions.

use_cases.title: Self-audit before submission
use_cases.description: Run automated checks and review rubric gaps before asking maintainers for a final review.

prompt_templates.title: Basic notebook audit
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a single notebook
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this Cookbook notebook: [notebook_path]. Run the automated checks and summarize the most important issues.

prompt_templates.title: Full rubric review
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit [notebook_path] against the Cookbook rubric. Include scoring for narrative quality, code quality, technical accuracy, and actionability.

prompt_templates.title: Focused security and setup check
prompt_templates.scenario: Targeted review of risky notebook areas
prompt_templates.prompt: Review [notebook_path] for hardcoded secrets, setup quality, dependency instructions, model constants, and output hygiene.

prompt_templates.title: Comparative editorial audit
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit [notebook_a] and [notebook_b]. Compare their rubric scores, identify shared weaknesses, and recommend publication order.

output: Strengths: clear problem framing, useful setup section, and concise code explanations.
Critical issues: no hardcoded secrets found.
Recommended improvements: expand the conclusion and add clearer learning objective mapping.

output_examples.input: Check examples/agents.ipynb for setup and security issues.
output: Automated checks found one critical issue and two warnings.
The notebook may contain a hardcoded credential in one code cell.
Setup warnings include noisy install output and a missing model constant.
Next step: remove the credential and rerun validation before manual review.

output_examples.input: Compare two draft Cookbook notebooks and recommend edits.
output: Notebook A is closer to publication because its narrative structure is complete.
Notebook B needs stronger prerequisites and more explanation after code cells.
Both notebooks should improve conclusion sections before final approval.

best_practices: Run the automated validator before starting the manual rubric review.
Read the style guide first so scoring uses the current Cookbook standards.
Give specific recommendations tied to notebook sections or cells.
anti_patterns: Skipping secret checks because the notebook is only a draft.
Scoring narrative quality without reading the markdown conversion.
Giving generic feedback that does not point to concrete notebook changes.

faq.question: What does this skill audit?
faq.answer: It audits Anthropic Cookbook notebooks for structure, clarity, code quality, security checks, and rubric alignment.

faq.question: Does it change the notebook?
faq.answer: No. It reads the notebook and creates a markdown review file for easier inspection.

faq.question: Does it run notebook cells?
faq.answer: No. It validates notebook content and structure, but it does not execute notebook code.

faq.question: Can it detect secrets?
faq.answer: Yes. It uses detect-secrets when available and falls back to basic pattern checks.

faq.question: Why does it need local command execution?
faq.answer: It calls local notebook conversion and secret scanning tools to prepare evidence for review.

faq.question: Is manual review still needed?
faq.answer: Yes. Automated checks help, but humans must judge pedagogy, accuracy, and usefulness.
